title: Allan govt unveils $14m rescue package as it braces for AI-driven job losses
full_text:
The Allan government is bracing for an onslaught of AI-driven job losses, scrambling to get ahead of the trend with a $14m to rescue package for Victorian workers whose livelihoods will be wiped out.
The Allan government’s AI Investment Package will plough $8.2m into a new career rescue scheme designed to support workers in at-risk industries.
The plan is to fast-track at-risk employees to work in specialist AI and technology roles before AI makes them redundant.
The move follows thousands of unexpected job losses across Victoria and Australia in the opening months of 2026, as the adoption of artificial intelligence accelerates through workplaces.
Last month, the Commissioner of Jobs and Skills Australia, Barney Glover, warned the data on the impact of AI was rapidly changing and that job losses were expected to rise beyond 4 per cent impacting more than the estimated 600,000 workers Australia-wide.
The deepest impacts are expected in white-collar professions, such as accounting, finance and legal fields, as well as customer service, retail, and logistics.
As part of the state package, a new Digital, AI and Technology TAFE Centre of Excellence will open at the Chisholm Institute campus in Frankston. It’s hoped the facility will be a lifeline to outer-suburban workers who might be left behind by the tech boom.
But while the government is spending to protect workers, the state has admitted it doesn't yet know the full financial fallout.
In parliamentary hearings this month, Department of Treasury and Finance officials confirmed the state has no financial modelling on how AI will hit Victoria’s payroll tax base, one of the government’s biggest revenue streams.
Treasury Secretary Chris Barrett told the hearing it was “too soon to tell” what the impact of lay-offs in the workforce would have, arguing that past technological shifts had typically spawned new jobs to replace the ones they erased.
Treasurer Jaclyn Symes moved to calm nerves across the public service, firmly ruling out using AI as a backdoor excuse to slash government jobs.
Ms Symes said the technology would be used to make existing public servants more productive rather than to cut headcount.
The budget papers laid bare just how dramatically AI was already reshaping some ways Victorians were interacting with government resources.
Visits to Victorian government websites have collapsed, plummeting from 157 million in 2024–25 to an expected 126 million in 2025–26, a staggering drop of 31 million visits in a single year.
Bureaucrats have blamed AI-powered search overviews now deployed by major search engines, which scrape government websites and serve the answers directly onto the results page, meaning Victorians are getting what they need without ever clicking through to government websites that have cost millions of dollars to create and run.
The state government have also revealed it will create a new AI Advisory Committee, charged with guiding the government on how to safely harness emerging technology.
Services Minister Ingrid Stitt, who will share oversight of the committee, however, admitted that planning for the body was still in its early stages, but pledged it would span a broad cross-section of Victoria.
She said the panel will bring together AI experts drawn from academia and industry, business representatives and, critically, worker representatives, with the Victorian Trades Hall Council and unions set to have a seat at the table to ensure the workforce is not left voiceless.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.

heading: Data Science and AI
